SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (that combining orthographic and vocabulary-wide semantic signals reduces over-segmentation) are supported by experiments showing improved F1 on English and Turkish and worse on Finnish (Section 5.1). The algorithmic formulation (rule sets, Radd/Rrep, and the optimization problem) is plausible and the paper acknowledges known limitations (greedy inference, concatenative morphology only). However, mathematical derivations are light and key experimental details (e.g., embedding training corpora, statistical significance tests) are missing, leaving some practical reproducibility and robustness questions unanswered.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces MORSE which integrates vocabulary-wide morpho-syntactic regularities from word representations (difference vectors) with orthographic cues; the authors claim this is the first to evaluate morphological relations via difference vectors rather than only local semantic relatedness. While it builds on known ideas (word embeddings, Morfessor/MDL frameworks and local semantic checks), the vocabulary-wide use of difference-vector consistency to validate affix rules represents a notable and non-trivial extension over prior work.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into system description, inference, experiments and discussion and defines key notation (e.g., Radd, Rrep) and the optimization objective. However the optimization formulation and hyperparameter thresholds are presented tersely, figures/tables are referenced but not fully explained in the provided text, and some portions are repetitive, making parts of the method and experimental setup harder to follow without additional detail.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments span multiple languages (English, Turkish, Finnish), use Morpho Challenge data and a newly introduced semantically driven dataset (SD17), include cross-validation on SD17, and compare to Morfessor and published state-of-the-art. The paper also studies hyperparameter sensitivity and tuning-data size dependency and discusses limitations. Still, important ablations (e.g., effect of different embeddings, threshold choices) and reproducibility details (exact embedding/training corpora and hyperparameter settings) are not fully presented.

IMPACT.justification: Morpheme segmentation is a core NLP task and the proposed integration of semantic vector-space regularities addresses a real shortcoming (over-segmentation) of dominant methods like Morfessor. The method improves results on some languages and introduces a compositionality-focused dataset, which could influence future work, though limitations on non-concatenative morphologies and lower performance on Finnish temper the claim of broad transformative impact.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (unsupervised/semi-supervised morpheme segmentation combining embeddings and orthography) is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope, the paper targets the right audience, and it uses methods and evaluation standards (Morpho Challenge, comparisons to Morfessor and recent literature) expected at top venues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares MORSE against the widely used Morfessor, Morfessor variants, and published state-of-the-art (e.g., Narasimhan et al.), and evaluates on standard (Morpho Challenge) and newly proposed datasets, using similar metrics (boundary-level, morpheme-level where appropriate). Comparisons are generally fair, but the paper lacks statistical significance testing and some potentially relevant baselines or ablations (e.g., different embedding types or recent neural segmentation models) are not fully explored in the provided text.
